roots.title: Midterm Exam Guidelines and Model Training Concepts
roots.summary: The midterm exam is scheduled for 10/23 from 7-9 pm, with specific instructions on seating assignments, required materials, restroom procedures, and policies on late arrivals. An extra office hour is available today from 1 to 2 p.m. The discussion on model training covers the distinction between seen and unseen data, emphasizing training on seen data and evaluating on unseen data. Key concepts include model complexity, underfitting, overfitting, and generalization. Cross-validation is introduced as a method to assess model performance, alongside the necessity of a test set to evaluate without bias, including dataset splitting techniques using scikit-learn. The segment also discusses splitting a dataset of 390 points into training, validation, and test sets, evaluating polynomial models of degrees 2, 3, and 4 using mean squared error (MSE) to select the best model, which is degree two due to optimal validation performance. It highlights the relationship between model complexity and validation MSE, addressing overfitting risks, and introduces K-fold cross-validation to reduce variability in validation error.

children.title: Midterm Exam Guidelines and Model Training Concepts
children.summary: The midterm exam is scheduled for tomorrow, 10/23, from 7-9 pm. Students must check their email for seating assignments, arrive on time, and bring writing utensils and their CalID, as calculators and cheat sheets are not permitted. A reference sheet will be provided. Restroom breaks require signing out with the exam, CalID, and phone, and no one can leave in the last 10 minutes. Late arrivals over an hour will not be allowed to take the exam. An additional office hour is available today from 1 to 2 p.m. The discussion on model training emphasizes the difference between seen and unseen data, with training on seen data and evaluation on unseen data. It covers model complexity, underfitting, and overfitting, defining generalization as a model's performance on unseen data. Cross-validation is introduced as a method to assess model performance. Additionally, it explains the necessity of a test set to evaluate model performance without bias, detailing how to split datasets using scikit-learn.

children.title: Midterm Exam Logistics and Guidelines
children.summary: The midterm exam is scheduled for tomorrow, 10/23, from 7-9 pm. Students should check their email for seating and room assignments and arrive on time to find seats. They need to bring writing utensils and their CalID, while calculators and cheat sheets are not allowed; a reference sheet will be provided. During the exam, restroom breaks require signing out with the exam, CalID, and phone. Students should raise their hands for questions, which may be shared with the class. No one can leave the exam room in the last 10 minutes, and late arrivals (over an hour) will not be allowed to take the exam. An additional office hour will be held today from 1 to 2 p.m. for any questions.

children.title: Midterm Logistics and Exam Preparation
children.summary: The midterm exam is scheduled for tomorrow, 10/23, from 7-9 pm. Students should check their email for seating and room assignments. It is important to arrive on time to find seats and prepare for the exam. Students should bring writing utensils and their CalID, but calculators and cheat sheets are not allowed as a reference sheet will be provided.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Good morning, NATO 100, another lecture. This is going to be a very fun lecture and kind of useful and is going to be a little bit practical. So you're going to use all of these topics that we're going to teach today a lot in future projects on homeworks that you're going to work on. So I'll go ahead and before I start the lecture, I'll just talk a little bit about some midterm logistics that's happening tomorrow. You all know that midterm is going to be tomorrow, You're sitting assignment has been emailed to you, so you should know the room or the seat number that is assigned to you. So make sure that you arrive at right at 7pm so that you have enough time to find your seat and also put away your stuff and then be able to receive the exam paper on time. Bring all the writing utensils that you want, bring your CalID and make sure that you We don't bring your calculator or any cheat sheet or whatnot, because we're going to be providing a reference sheet to you. children.title: Midterm Exam Logistics and Reference Sheet
children.summary: A reference sheet for the midterm has been posted, which includes key information for study. The midterm is scheduled for Wednesday, 10/23, from 7-9 pm, and students should check their email for seating and room assignments. Arriving by 7 pm is crucial for finding seats. During the exam, students can use the restroom by signing out with their exam, CalID, and phone. Questions during the exam can be directed to proctors or instructors, who will determine if clarifications should be shared with the class.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: We also posted a reference sheet for the midterm. So if you want to study the things that you need to remember, you will see in the reference sheet on the exam or in the midterm tomorrow. Take a look at the reference sheet that was posted. I think both on Ed and is also on the website. So that is available to you. Some other logistics about the exam is that you are allowed to use restroom. If you want to, the logistics is that you would come to the Proctor, you would give your exam, your CalID, and also your phone. If you have your phone on you, and then you will be able to sign out. So you can go and then come back, grab your exam paper again, and go to your seat. We are asking you that if you have any questions during the exam, raise your hand. One of the Proctors or one of the professors is going to come to you. We will actually be in most of the rooms, I mean me and Professor Gonzales, and you should be able to ask us questions. Any question that you ask, the proctor is going to ask instructors and then we'll decide whether we want to make a class wide or broad clarification or not. Video: 1: Lec 16 - DS100 Fa24 - Cross-Va X

quietly for the remaining time.

children.title: Midterm Exam Logistics and Guidelines
children.summary: The midterm exam is scheduled for Wednesday, 10/23, from 7-9 pm. Students should check their email for seating and room assignments and are advised to arrive by 7 pm to find their seats. Essential items to bring include a writing utensil and Cal ID, while calculators and cheat sheets are not permitted. During the exam, if a student needs to use the bathroom, they must take their exam paper, phone, and Cal ID to the proctors. For clarifications, students should raise their hands; class-wide clarifications will be projected on the screen. Additionally, students are not allowed to leave the exam room in the last 10 minutes, and late arrivals (more than one hour after the start) will not be permitted to take the exam.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: If you're not making clarification, that means that the response to your question is on the exam or is the response to the question that you're writing. If we, in fact, find any issues based on your question, we will make a classified clarification that's going to be on the screen so you should be able to see all the clarifications that we're making classified on the screen as well. We are asking you to not leave the exam room in the last 10 minutes, so you're free to leave beforehand, but in the last 10 minutes we want everyone to be able to concentrate on the exam and not be distracted by a lot of people leaving. We are also not letting people to start the exam one hour after the initial start time. Because there might be cases that the students might finish early and then leave and then students taking the exam after the first person left the room is not going to be possible. So that's why we have the hard cutoff of if you arrive late more than an hour into the exam, you're not able to take the exam. Video: 1: Lec 16 - DS100 Fa24 - Cross-Va X

children.title: Midterm Logistics and Upcoming Topics
children.summary: Students are encouraged to reach out through the midterm accommodation form for any questions or concerns about the exam. An additional office hour will be held today from 1 to 2 p.m. in office 775, alongside Professor Gonzales' office hour from 1 to 3 p.m. The midterm is scheduled for Wednesday, 10/23, from 7-9 p.m., with seating assignments emailed to students. Arrive by 7 p.m. with a writing utensil and Cal ID; calculators and cheat sheets are not allowed. During the exam, bring your exam paper, phone, and Cal ID for bathroom breaks, and raise your hand for clarification questions. Today's topic will cover cross-validation and regularization.

children.title: Model Training, Complexity, and Generalization
children.summary: The segment discusses model training and the distinction between seen and unseen data sets, emphasizing that training occurs on scene data while evaluation is on unseen data. It highlights the relationship between model complexity, training error, and variance, explaining underfitting as a result of overly simple models and overfitting as a consequence of excessive complexity. Generalization is defined as a model's ability to perform well on unseen data, aiming for minimal training error without underfitting or overfitting. The importance of cross-validation is introduced as a method to evaluate model performance by simulating unseen data from the training dataset.

children.title: Model Training and Complexity Discussion
children.summary: The segment discusses the concepts of training and unseen data sets, emphasizing that training is performed on scene data points while model accuracy is evaluated on unseen data points. It highlights the importance of understanding the distinction between seen and unseen data, as well as the implications for model performance. Additionally, it revisits the topic of model complexity, noting that increased complexity typically leads to decreased training error due to the model's enhanced capacity to capture data patterns.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: We basically told you that there is a concept of scene data set or data points and then unseen data points that we train on the scene data points, we call that a training set and then we test the model efficiency or accuracy or mean square error or root mean square error on the unseen data set and then we would report the model accuracy based on that unseen data set. And the concept of what is seen, what is unseen, and how we can come up with a way to kind of have a sense of what the general data that we have not trained on is going to look like. It's going to be something that we're going to talk about today. We're also going to talk a little bit about model complexity. That's the thing that we started seeing in the previous lecture, not previous lecture, on lecture 14, which we did talk about this plot. This was the plot towards the end of the last lecture. We said that as the model complexity increases, the training error is going to decrease because the model's capacity in order to model for the pattern in the data is going to substantially increase. children.title: Underfitting vs Overfitting in Model Complexity
children.summary: As model complexity increases, training error decreases, but variance increases due to many moving parts in the model. Underfitting occurs when the model is too simple to capture the underlying trends in the data, while overfitting happens when the model becomes too complex, fitting noise rather than the actual trends. The relationship between model complexity, training error, and variance is crucial in understanding these concepts.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So the training error is going to decrease always as the model complex to the increases. But the other thing that is going to happen at the same time is that the variance is going to keep increasing. The variance is going to keep increasing because there is going to be a lot of different moving parts that with the small changes in one of those moving parts then substantially the model prediction is going to change. as the model prediction is going to change, then that is going to mean that the variance in prediction is going to be very high. So that's the concept of underfitting versus overfitting. So when the mother complexity is a small meaning that the model is simple, we have not captured the model, the pattern in our data says that we're underfitting the actual underlying trend in the data, but as the model is getting complicated and as the variance is going to increase, we would say that we overfitted to the training data. We may have fitted to the noise in the training data, not the actual trend in the data. Video: 1: Lec 16 - DS100 Fa24 - Cross-Va X

children.title: Model Generalization and Cross Validation Overview
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the concepts of generalization, underfitting, and overfitting in model training. Generalization refers to a model's ability to perform well on unseen datasets without fitting to noise in the training data. The optimal scenario is to achieve a reasonable training error without underfitting or overfitting, while maintaining some variance. The lecture will also cover cross validation, which is essential for evaluating model performance by simulating unseen data from the provided training dataset.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So our model is not going to be generalizable. Generalizable, it means that if you give me an unseen dataset, because I did not fit to the noise in my training data, I'm able to generalize and I'm able to make equally good production on the unseen dataset. So that's kind of the way that we're seeing underfitting versus overfitting. And our optimum point is going to be that we want to be at this suite of spot, meaning that we don't want to be underfitting, we don't want to be overfitting. We want to have a reasonable training error. So this is the reasonable training error that we're at. And then we also want to have some variance, but not high variance. So that's the topic that we're going to talk about today, but in the second half of today's lecture. But initially I want to talk a little bit about cross validation in the first half. Cross validation is a concept that you need in order to make sure that how do you do model evaluation in the first place. If you have a training data that is provided to you, how can you make sure that you can kind of simulate for unseen data set and then use those data sets or data points in order to evaluate the efficiency of the model. children.title: Cross-Validation and Model Complexity Overview
children.summary: The segment introduces key concepts related to cross-validation and model complexity, emphasizing the importance of understanding these terms in the context of training models in data science. It discusses the example of a vehicle dataset where model performance is evaluated for predicting miles per gallon based on features like horsepower. The complexity of models is highlighted, noting that with only five data points, even a polynomial model of degree four can be considered complex, raising concerns about overfitting.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And then again, in the second half, I'm going to talk about how to control for model complexity and modern considerations for model complexity, which is the topic that is referred to as regularization. So let's go into the first thing that we need to know with cross with the cross validation concept. And that's that some terminology that we need to get familiar with and also know the process of the actual training that's going to be in every data science lifecycle that you're going to train any model. and report model performance on. So this is what we had seen in a previous lecture. So we had seen that, okay, if we have a vehicle dataset and we want to go ahead and model for ModperGallon having some information about the cars, for example, here we had horsepower as the feature. And if we go ahead and say that, oh, I have five data points here, these are the five data points that I have. And if I go ahead and train a complex model, And a complex model in this case could be that, okay, with five data points, even a degree four or triple enumil is going to be a complex model, because you only have five data points. children.title: Overfitting, Test Sets, and Design Matrix Creation
children.summary: Complex models can perfectly fit training data, leading to overfitting, where the model fails to generalize to unseen data. To evaluate model performance, a test set is essential, serving as unseen data completely excluded from training decisions to avoid bias. The test set is used only once after training to compute evaluation metrics like accuracy, MSE, or RMSE. The dataset is split into a training set and a test set, with scikit-learn providing a function to automate this process. The design matrix is created using features such as horsepower and its polynomial powers, with mile per gallon as the target variable.

children.title: Overfitting in Complex Models
children.summary: A complex model can fit training data perfectly, as demonstrated by a degree four polynomial that matches all five data points. However, this often leads to overfitting, meaning the model fails to generalize to unseen data, which does not follow the same pattern as the training set. To evaluate model performance on test data, it is essential to separate a portion of the dataset for testing.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So technically a degree, triple enumil, or a degree four, whole enumil can perfectly match the entire five data points that you have and pass through them perfectly. Which is what you're seeing and the left happening that the model is so complex that it tried to actually fit perfectly to the data And it can actually do that because it's complex. And then what's happening on the other hand is that, if we go ahead and send the entire data set that we have in order to do testing and see how the model is performing, you can see that that model is just nonsense. That's not the pattern in our data set. So that's an example that we're saying that the model has overfitted to the training data, but is not generalizable on the unseen data set. And the unseen data set, in this case, is anything that we have not trained on, which is the rest of the data points that we had. So in order to find out how we can model for the test data, what we can do is we can separate out a portion of our data set. Video: 1: Lec 16 - DS100 Fa24 - Cross-Va X

children.title: Using Test Sets in Model Training
children.summary: A test set is crucial in the training pipeline as it serves as unseen data to evaluate model performance. During training, the test set is completely excluded from any decisions regarding feature engineering or optimization to avoid bias. After training is complete, the model is tested on the test set only once to ensure that no information from the test set influenced the training process. This approach maintains the integrity of the evaluation, as any changes made based on test set performance would compromise the model's validity.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And we would say that that's my test data. That's the data that I'm going to simulate as an unseen dataset. And I'm going to use it as an external dataset. And I will do the training on the remainder of the data points that I have. So our training pipeline is going to be that we're going to separate out a test set. We would say that we do not consider this test set during any part of our training. So any decision that we're making during the training, feature engineering, optimization, during none of those stages, we're going to consider this test set. But we do it on the rest of the data points that we have. And then what we'll do is afterwards, after we're done with the entire training, what we'll do is we'll test it on the test set only once. The reason that it's only once is that we're under the assumption or obligation that based on the performance of the model on the test set, we cannot make any changes in the training because otherwise, the model or the modeling process have used some information about the test set in its process and we don't want that. children.title: Test Set Usage in Model Training
children.summary: A test set is a portion of the dataset set aside exclusively for testing the model's performance, ensuring it is not used during the training phase. The test set is utilized only once to compute evaluation metrics such as accuracy, MSE, or RMSE after model fine-tuning is completed. The process involves separating the dataset into a training set and a test set, with a function in scikit-learn that automates this split by requiring the design matrix and target variable inputs, along with the desired percentage for the test set.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: In order to make sure that it's an entirely held out set, it just needs to make sure that we only test or use the test set only once. And then we will basically report it, report the accuracy, MSE or RMSC or whatever that evaluation metric is, and this test set and then we'll be done with our training. So the pipeline is going to look something like this. You have an entire data set that is provided to you as a data set that you're going to be doing modeling on because there is no way for you to gather additional data points with the same kind of structure that are given that is giving to you in the in the data set. Then we will go ahead and say that I'm going to separate out its set. I'm going to call that a test set and I'm going to use the remainder as a training set. I'll train in the training set and then I'll test it on the test set and then I'll report that accuracy. There's an actual function trained test to split an SK learned that will do that automatically for us. That we don't need to be worried about how we do this split. What we just need to do is we need to provide the design matrix we need to provide the why protection vector or the target variables and or the target values and then we will define what percentage of our data points need to be in the in the validation in the test set and it will go ahead and split it for us as well. children.title: Design Matrix Creation and Test Set Definition
children.summary: The design matrix is created using horsepower and its polynomial powers (up to the fourth power) as features, while mile per gallon is the target variable for prediction. Additionally, a test set is defined as a portion of the dataset set aside for testing purposes, which is not used during model training. The workflow involves performing a train-test split, designing the model using only the training set, and evaluating model performance on the test set.

children.title: Train-Test Split and Model Evaluation in sklearn
children.summary: The segment covers the use of the train_test_split function from sklearn.model_selection for splitting data into training and testing sets, emphasizing the need to pass both feature variables (X) and target variables (y) for correspondence. It sets the test size to 20% and discusses the role of the random_state parameter for reproducibility. The importance of controlling randomness in splits is highlighted, with a fixed random seed ensuring consistent results. The sizes of the dataset, training set, and test set are printed for confirmation. The segment also touches on fitting a model with the training set and evaluating its performance on the test set, noting potential variations in model performance.

children.title: Train-Test Split Using sklearn
children.summary: The segment discusses the use of the train_test_split function from the sklearn.model_selection module for splitting data into training and testing sets. It emphasizes the importance of passing both feature variables (X) and target variables (y) to ensure a one-to-one correspondence between them in the training and test sets. Additionally, it explains setting the test size to 20% of the dataset and mentions that the random_state parameter helps in making results reproducible. The segment also introduces transformations of the 'hp' variable to predict a car's 'mpg'.

children.title: Controlling Randomness in Train-Test Splits
children.summary: To ensure replicable train-test splits in machine learning, a fixed random state (e.g., an integer like 100) can be set. This random seed generates consistent random indices for selecting data points, allowing for the same training and testing datasets across different runs. The train_test_split function from sklearn.model_selection is used, where 'test_size' defines the proportion of data for testing, and 'shuffle' randomizes the data before splitting. The sizes of the full dataset, training set, and test set can be printed to confirm the split.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: The other thing that we can do is we could say that, okay, I know that you're going to randomly is split my training data into two pieces. But I want you to do it in a way that it's replicable. So the next time that I come and run this notebook, I get exactly the same training data and the same test data. I want to get the same as splits. So in order to control for that, a lot of times, we would say that we're going to say that my random state is going to be a fixed kind of integer, that that integer is going to be a fixed integer. start with the random seed of 100. And this is kind of outside of the scope for data 100 how we generate random numbers but just know that this random function is going to basically take a seed take an integer and just based on that integer create a bunch of different indices or different values and those values are going to be used for us to decide which row is going to be in or out in or out. So those are random kind of values that we're going to create. Video: 1: Lec 16 - DS100 Fa24 - Cross-Va X

children.title: Train-Test Split and Model Evaluation
children.summary: The segment discusses the process of performing a train-test split using the train_test_split function from the sklearn.model_selection module. It highlights the importance of parameters like test_size, which determines the proportion of data for testing, and random_state, which ensures reproducibility. The output indicates the sizes of the full dataset, training set, and test set. Additionally, it mentions fitting a model using the training set and evaluating its performance through predictions on the test set, noting that the model's performance may vary.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So here I use just 100 as random so that we can replicate this process. So let's see the output. So the output here is telling me that the first thing that is generated has 313 rows and four columns. It kind of gives me a sense that it's a portion of my training data or my design matrix because it has four columns looking at all of those four features that I have. And you can see that it has multiple different rows. And the row, for example, with label 72s remaining, with 89, with 92, these are our remaining. And you can kind of see that there is also another data frame that's created here again, but now this time with 79 rows. And you can kind of also see that we also have the same kind of split on the Y values. So the Y value was my pergallance. So that's the same series object. leaf_chunk.audio: So here I use just 100 as random so that we can replicate this process. So let's see the output. So the output here is telling me that the first thing that is generated has 313 rows and four columns. It kind of gives me a sense that it's a portion of my training data or my design matrix because it has four columns looking at all of those four features that I have. And you can see that it has multiple different rows. And the row, for example, with label 72s remaining, with 89, with 92, these are our remaining. And you can kind of see that there is also another data frame that's created here again, but now this time with 79 rows. And you can kind of also see that we also have the same kind of split on the Y values. So the Y value was my pergallance. So that's the same series object. So it's basically telling me that, oh, for the same bros, remember, it was 728992, these are the corresponding by values.

children.title: Data Splitting for Model Training
children.summary: The dataset consists of 392 data points, which are split into training and testing sets, with 80% allocated for training and 20% for testing. The training data frame is labeled as X_train, while the testing data frame is labeled as X_test. Additionally, corresponding labels for training and testing are stored as Y_train and Y_test. The sizes of the resulting datasets are confirmed, with 313 data points in the training set and 79 in the testing set.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So the number of them is again, 313. And there's also another one that is 79. So it looks like that it's trying to split. So the ratio between them is 8220. So that's kind of splitting my data set into two pieces. But what I want to do is I want to be able to store these into variables that then I can use those variables to do the model training. So I will basically go ahead and call this that, okay, the first thing that you returned to me, it sounds like that it was the training data frame. So I'm gonna call that X underscore train. The second thing, that was also a form of a data frame. It sounds like that that was the 20% that I wanted to split out. So I'm gonna call that X underscore test and then also Y train and also Y-HES. And I'm gonna store that and we can basically take a look at the sizes. Originally the size of the dataset was 392 data points, 80% went to training, 20% went to test, so now I have my trained test displayed automatically for me. children.title: Linear Regression Model Training and Evaluation Process
children.summary: The training of a linear regression model involves dividing the dataset into a training set and a test set, using the 'train_test_split' function to specify test size and random state for reproducibility. The training set (313 points) is used to fit the model, while the test set (79 points) is reserved for evaluation, with mean squared error calculated to assess performance. Training error was noted at 18 units and test error at 21 units, emphasizing the importance of not altering the model after evaluating test data to maintain its integrity. The segment also introduces validation sets, which allow for internal testing during training, enabling adjustments like feature engineering until satisfactory performance is achieved before final testing on the actual test data.

children.title: Linear Regression Model Training and Evaluation
children.summary: The process of training a linear regression model involves separating the dataset into a training set and a test set. The training set is used to fit the model, utilizing X-Train and Y-Train. The model's performance is evaluated by calculating the mean squared error between the training data and the predictions. The dataset is split using the 'train_test_split' function, specifying the test size and random state for reproducibility. The sizes of the full dataset, training set, and test set are printed, indicating 392 total points, with 313 in the training set and 79 in the test set.

children.title: Model Evaluation and Test Data Limitations
children.summary: Training and test errors were discussed, with training error calculated at 18 units and test error at 21 units. It was emphasized that no modifications to the model should be made after evaluating test data, as it serves as a holdout set. The importance of maintaining the integrity of the test data for unbiased performance assessment was highlighted. Additionally, the introduction of validation sets was mentioned as a method to fine-tune models by splitting the training set into a validation set and a mini training set.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So that's the training error and then I'll do the same thing on the test data. So Y test and also the prediction on those test data points that I had. So I'll get the X-Train Y-Train and you can see that my training error is going to be 18 units and my test error is now going to be 21. So what do I do if I'm not happy with 21? What do you think I can do? Can I go back and change anything in my model and then come back and see if I get a better results. So for example, I think horsepower to the power of five is that going to be acceptable? It's not going to be acceptable there is no decision or refinement and modeling i can do after i do the testing on the test data if you're not happy with the test data You can do anything about it so that's the issue with the test data it always needs to be a hold out that you cannot make any decision or cannot refine anything in your modeling process after seeing the performance on the test data. children.title: Using Validation Sets for Model Assessment
children.summary: The segment discusses the importance of creating an internal test set, referred to as a validation set, for evaluating model performance during the training process. It explains that if the model's performance is unsatisfactory on the validation set, adjustments can be made, such as feature engineering, followed by re-testing. This cycle can continue until satisfactory performance is achieved. Once the model performs well on the validation set, it is exported and tested on the actual test data only once. The validation set allows for multiple tests as it is part of the training data.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So, what we will do with this issue is that, again, if we're not happy, we're stuck. But the other thing that we can do is we can say that what if I create an internal test set? So I can make decisions, I can take a look at the performance of the model and the internal test set. And if I was not happy, I'll go ahead and do something with my modeling process, I change features, I do more feature engineering, come back tested and the internal test set. And I'll do this cycle as many times as I want to, up until the point that I'm happy with the performance and my internal test data. Once I'm happy with the performance on the internal test data, then I'll export the model, then I'll only test it on the actual test data only once. So we call this internal test data a validation set. So we use it for validating our modeling process, and we can actually do inference on it or test on it multiple times, because it is part of our training data. children.title: Splitting Data for Training and Validation
children.summary: The modeling pipeline involves splitting the full dataset into training and test sets. The training data is further divided into a smaller training set (referred to as training mini) and a validation set. This is achieved by using a function to split the training data, allocating 20% for validation. It is emphasized that introducing a validation set is crucial to avoid adjusting the model based on the test set, which would compromise its ability to represent performance on unseen data.

children.title: Model Training, Validation, and Cross-Validation Techniques
children.summary: The segment discusses splitting a dataset of 390 points into training (313 points) and validation sets (63 points), with 79 points reserved for testing. It evaluates polynomial models of degrees 2, 3, and 4 using mean squared error (MSE) to select the best model, which is found to be the degree two polynomial due to its optimal validation performance. As model complexity increases, validation MSE tends to rise, highlighting the importance of the validation set in determining model complexity. The risk of overfitting is addressed, noting that while training error decreases with complexity, validation error initially decreases and then increases. K-fold cross validation is introduced as a method to mitigate variability in validation error by using multiple data chunks as internal test sets, ensuring each data point is included in the validation process at least once, thus improving the reliability of model performance assessment.

children.title: Model Training, Validation, and Complexity Analysis
children.summary: The segment covers the process of splitting a dataset of 390 data points into training and validation sets, with 313 points for training (254 for mini training and 63 for validation) and 79 points for testing. It discusses training models with polynomial degrees of 2, 3, and 4, calculating mean squared error (MSE) to evaluate performance. The best model is selected based on validation performance, revealing that a degree two polynomial performs best, as increased complexity does not guarantee improved validation performance. The analysis indicates that as model complexity rises, validation MSE tends to increase, emphasizing the importance of the validation set in determining optimal model complexity. Further testing of polynomial models from degrees 0 to 18 will be conducted to visualize changes in validation error.

children.title: Model Training and Validation Set Split
children.summary: Variables for training and validation sets are defined, with the original dataset containing 390 data points. After splitting, 79 points are allocated for testing, leaving 313 points for further division into a mini training set of 254 points and a validation set of 63 points. The process involves training models with polynomial degrees of 2, 3, and 4 to evaluate performance on the validation set. Additionally, the mean squared error for training and test sets is calculated, providing insights into model performance.

children.title: Model Selection and Validation Performance
children.summary: The process of selecting the best model involves validating its performance on the validation set before testing on the actual test data. The output includes a plot of validation points and the mean squared error (MSE) for different polynomial degrees. The analysis shows that a degree two polynomial performs best on the validation data, indicating that increasing model complexity does not necessarily improve performance on validation data, despite better training data performance.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And once I'm happy, once I decide which order is going to be good, and performs better on the validation set, then that's going to be your model, then you're going to ship that model to the test data so that you can see the performance of it on the test data. And hopefully this time, it will do better than... What we had seen before which was 21 unit of I think it was MSU armacy on the test date and we'll take a look at that as well. So let's take a look at the output. The output is the plot of all of the validation points. We also plotted the msc of the validation data and then you can kind of see the fit of each one of these degree polynomial, degree two polynomial, triple and a male and four polynomial. And then you can see that the best performance that we're getting on the tray on the validation data is actually degree two polynomial. We did not need to go to to degree three and four. Meaning that although the performance might get better on the training data, always as you increase the model complexity, the performance on the training data is gonna get better. children.title: Model Complexity and Validation Performance Analysis
children.summary: As model complexity increases, the performance on the validation data tends to drop, indicated by an increasing Mean Squared Error (MSE). The validation set is crucial for determining the appropriate complexity of the model and identifying useful features. The analysis will involve testing polynomial models of degrees ranging from 0 to 18 to observe how validation error changes with model complexity. A plot will be generated to visualize the results, showing the MSE for each degree of polynomial fitted to the validation data.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But as you're seeing here is that as I'm increasing the complexity of the model, the performance on the validation data is dropping because the MSC is increasing, right? So this is the point that the validation set is very helpful for us to figure out the complexity of the model or what model features are going to be useful for the modeling purposes. So we take degree two polynomial, then we actually test this model on the test data, and we should be able to get the result. So I'm going to go one step further, and I would go ahead and say that what if I go ahead and check the model performance on all of the degree polynomials between 0 and 18? To kind of see that if it's increasing, if the model complexity increases, what happens with the validation set? And is the validation error going to be influenced differently for different kind of complexity of the models? So let's take a look and this is the plot that we'll get. from sklearn.preprocessing import PolynomialFeatures
def fit_model_dataset(degree):
pipelined_model = Pipeline([
('polynomial_transformation', PolynomialFeatures(degree)),

])
pipelined_model. fit(X_train_mini[["hp"]], Y_train_mini)
return mean_squared_error(Y_val, pipelined_model.predict(X_val[["hp"]]))
errors = [fit_model_dataset(degree) for degree in range(0, 18)]
MSEs_and_k = pd.DataFrame({"k": range(0, 18), "MSE": errors})
plt.figure(dpi=120)
plt.plot(range(0, 18), errors)
plt.xlabel("Model Complexity (degree of polynomial)")
plt.ylabel("Validation MSE")
plt.xticks(range(0, 18));
MSEs_and_k.rename(columns={"k" :"Degree"}) . set_index("Degree")

children.title: Model Complexity and Validation Error Analysis
children.summary: The discussion focuses on how model complexity, represented by polynomial degrees, affects validation Mean Squared Error (MSE). Starting from a constant model with high validation MSE, adding features improves the model's ability to capture data patterns, resulting in lower MSE. The MSE decreases significantly with a degree two polynomial, but shows a slight increase from degrees three to four, indicating a potential overfitting risk. Beyond degree eleven, further complexity leads to fitting noise rather than generalizable patterns, causing validation error to rise. The goal is to identify the optimal complexity where validation error is minimized.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: On the x-axis, you're seeing that the model complexities or degrees of the polynomial is starting from zero, which is going to be constant model, has a high validation MSE. And as we add features, obviously, you're able to model for the pattern in the data a little bit better. So we'd agree one polynomial, the MSE drops with degree two polynomial. It drops further. You're seeing that there's a slight increase between from two to three and four. That's what we also thought that it was slightly more. It was 16 points something. So it's getting a little bit more and then it hits a plateau up until the grip polynomial 11. And that's the point that it says that, okay, beyond this point, if you keep increasing the complexity, you're going to fit to the noise and the training data. And that's not generalizable pattern to the validation data. So the validation error is going to increase. So that's kind of the general thing that you're expecting to see with the complexity of the model. And again, we're trying to find the best point on this plot that the validation error is a small. children.title: Model Complexity, Overfitting, and Cross-Validation Techniques
children.summary: As model complexity increases, training error decreases while validation error initially decreases and then increases, highlighting the risk of overfitting. It is essential to find a balance that ensures good performance on both training and validation data for generalizability. K-full cross validation is introduced as a method to use multiple data chunks as internal test sets, reducing variability in validation error that can occur with random sampling. This method allows for a more reliable assessment of model performance by repeatedly extracting different subsets of training data. Additionally, using validation folds ensures that each data point from the training set is included in the validation process at least once, enhancing the representativeness of the validation set.

children.title: Model Complexity and Overfitting Analysis
children.summary: As model complexity increases, training error decreases, but validation error initially decreases and then starts to increase, indicating overfitting. It is crucial to find a balance where the model performs well on both training and validation data, ensuring generalizability to unseen data.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And then we can use that complexity as the complexity of our model. And then we can use that in order to build a model and then test it on the actual test data that we have. So let's go back and take a look at this, which is exactly kind of showing what we saw from the previous lecture that again, as complexity increases, the training error always decreases. But what we're seeing this time is that the validation error is going to always decrease, and then there is a point that it will start increasing. That's the point that we actually hit that suite of spot that after this point, what we're seeing is that we're overfitting to the training error. So the training error, we're seeing that it's decreasing, but now my validation error keeps increasing. And we really want to make sure that we are at the suite of spot that the model performance is good enough on the training data, but also good enough on the validation data. But also the model performance is generalizable to the two data points that are not our training data. Video: 1: Lec 16 - DS100 Fa24 - Cross-Va X

children.title: Model Complexity and Validation Error Analysis
children.summary: Model complexity influences training and validation errors, with training error decreasing as complexity increases. Initially, validation error decreases but eventually increases, indicating the need to control complexity to avoid overfitting. The goal is to find the optimal complexity 'sweet spot.' The upcoming discussion will focus on systematically connecting the training process with validation and test set evaluations.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So we just want to make sure that we're not on this other side of this validation line that has higher error under validation and then lower error for the training. to take a look at all of these different moving pieces and actually look into how to quantify them in the next two lectures, but kind of a summary of what's happening here is that always with model model complexity increasing the training error decreases. we talked about variance increasing, we talked about error under validation, first decreasing, and then it will increase in our goal is to control the model complexity so that we find the exact suite spot that we want. And then second half of today's lecture, I'm going to talk about how to find that sweetest spot systematically, but just know that this is why we need to control complexity. So before I jump into the second part, there is a topic that I want to talk about that's about how to systematically connect the training process, the internal testing or the validation that you have as well as testing on the test set. children.title: K-Full Cross Validation Explained
children.summary: K-full cross validation is a systematic process that utilizes multiple chunks of data as internal test sets instead of relying on a single sample. This approach mitigates the risk of variability in validation error that can arise from randomly selecting a small percentage of data (e.g., 20%) for testing. By repeatedly extracting different subsets of the training data, the validation error can be assessed more reliably across iterations, providing a more comprehensive evaluation of model performance.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And this process, this systematic process is called k-full cross validation. The idea behind k-full cross validation is that you're only having one internal test set. You're only giving yourself one time chance to see some example of unseen data set. But actually, what you can do is that you can, you grab a chunk of your data, you treat that as internal test set. But there are multiple different chunks of 20%, for example, data that you can extract from your training data. So why limit yourself to only one chunk? Specifically, the issue is going to be that if you're going to randomly select 20 samples and limit yourself to that exact 20% sample that you extracted, just an example, there is kind of a chance that you get lucky or you get unlucky in that only one shot that you have. So for example, here, but I have is that randomly selecting 20%, again randomly selecting 20%, again randomly selecting 20%, do the training, tested on that specific kind of random set of validation data that we extracted, but you can see that the validation error is very different from one iteration to another iteration. children.title: Cross-Validation and Data Folds
children.summary: To enhance model evaluation, it's important to systematically include each data point from the training set in the internal test set. This can be achieved through the use of validation folds, which allows for multiple segments of the training data to serve as validation sets. Instead of only reserving the last portion of the data for validation, one can utilize various chunks of the data, ensuring that each data point is included in the validation set at least once. This approach helps mitigate the risk of selecting a non-representative validation set.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So we don't want to leave everything to luck, but we want to make sure that we're systematically letting each data point in our training set to at least once sit in the internal test set. And that's why what we can do is systematically always keeping multiple different kinds of validation of spools in our training pipeline. So these chunks that we're looking at, these are called folds, right? So for example, what I've showed you previously was that, okay, take your training data, keep a percentage of that for training mini and keep a percentage for validation. But I always showed that as only keeping the last 20% or last x% of data to be your validation set. But in fact, what you can do is you can go ahead and grab the first chunk, the second chunk, the third chunk and the fourth chunk if your folds are going to be 20 percent size. This will let each one of the data points in your entire data set to be able to sit in the validation set at least once. Video: 1: Lec 16 - DS100 Fa24 - Cross-Va X

children.title: K-Fold Cross Validation Methodology and Error Analysis
children.summary: K-fold cross validation is a systematic method for validating a dataset by dividing it into multiple folds, where 'K' indicates the number of folds. For example, with a 20% validation chunk, five folds are created. Each fold is used as a validation set once while the remaining folds serve as the training set, ensuring comprehensive validation. The process includes randomly shuffling data and splitting it into K folds based on the validation percentage, allowing for error computation on each validation fold. The average of these errors, known as cross-validation error, provides insight into model performance, preventing bias from a single validation set.

children.title: K-Fold Cross Validation Explained
children.summary: K-fold cross validation is a systematic method for validating a dataset by dividing it into multiple folds. The 'K' represents the number of folds; for instance, with a validation chunk of 20% of the data, there would be five chunks of 20%. In this method, one chunk is used for validation while the remaining chunks serve as the training set. This process is repeated for each chunk, ensuring that every chunk is used as a validation set exactly once, resulting in the model being trained and validated five times.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And then nothing is going to be left to chance because there's always those different kinds of validations that you have, right? And you're systematically checking the data set or the training pipeline that you have under multiple different combinations of training data points and also validation data points. So that's called K-fold cross validation. But K-fold cross validation, we say that we're going to divide our entire data set into multiple folds. And K stands for how many folds you have here. So for example, if I'm going to say that my validation chunk is going to be 20% of my data, I will have five chunks of 20%. Right? And then what I can do is I can say that what I'll do is I will perform this algorithm, this systematic algorithm that's called cross validation that every time one chunk is going to be validation, the residue chunks are going to be training and then I will move on to the other chunks one at a time and then I'll perform the model link in this case is going to be five times because I have five chunks each one of them should be my validation set at least or exactly once. children.title: K-Fold Cross Validation Explained
children.summary: K-Full Cross Validation involves randomly shuffling data and splitting it into K folds based on the desired percentage for validation. For instance, with a 10% validation fold, there would be 10 folds, while a 20% fold results in 5 folds. The model is trained on all folds except the selected validation fold, and the model error is computed for that validation fold. This process is repeated for each of the K folds, allowing for comprehensive validation across the dataset.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So this K-Full Cross Validation, if I want to systematically explain what's happening is that you divide your data, you randomly shuffle your data point, right? Again, what you will do is after you randomize your data set, you will split it into K-folds, depending on what percentage of your data points are going to be in validations at every time. So for example, if your validation fold is going to be 10%, you will have 10 folds. Effectively, you're doing 10 fold cross validation. In my example, my fold percentage was 20%, so I'm having five fold cross validation. So I'm dividing it into K number of faults. I will train the model on every fold, other than the validation fold. I'll keep the validation fold out. What I'll do is I will compute the model error on the validation fold and I will record it. Then what happens is that I'll repeat this thing for all of the K-folds that I have. Video: 1: Lec 16 - DS100 Fa24 - Cross-Va X

children.title: Cross-Validation Error and K-Fold Method
children.summary: Different models are tested on various validation sets, leading to different errors, which is crucial for understanding model performance. The average of these errors is referred to as cross-validation error, ensuring the model is not biased by relying on a single validation set. In K-Fold Cross-Validation, one fold is selected as the validation fold while the model is trained on the remaining folds. The error is computed on the validation fold and recorded, allowing for a comprehensive evaluation across all folds.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So I'll get basically multiple different models. and different models are going to be tested on different validation sets and they're going to lead to different errors. And we're going to talk about why that's important and why that's actually helpful in our process. We use the term cross validation error as the average of the error of all of these models. Right? This is the way that we can make sure that our model is not biased, is not going to report only one validation error based on only one validation set. But now we are reporting the average of all of the validation errors from all of the validation folds that we have had. So again, what you can see is that this time for example I'm using the last fold as my validation fold I'll go ahead and train the model on the rest of the data and then I'll compute the MSc or whatever metric that you want to evaluate on the validation. Set and then I will record that validation error. I'll go through the rest of the defaults. Video: 1: Lec 16 - DS100 Fa24 - Cross-Va X

children.title: K-Fold Cross-Validation Explained
children.summary: K-fold cross-validation involves splitting a dataset into K folds, where one fold is used as the validation set while the model is trained on the remaining K-1 folds. The model's error is computed for the validation fold, and this process is repeated for each fold. The final cross-validation error is the average of the errors from all K folds. This method is crucial as it allows researchers to validate their models on data that was not used during training, thus providing a reliable measure of model accuracy when publishing research.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I'll do exactly the same thing. Train on the training part and then evaluate on the validation set that I have and then we are recording all of these validation errors and then cross validation error at the end is going to be the average of all of these validation errors that we had across all of those five models that we have had. So why is it helpful? Why am I doing this? It's important because now what's happening is that my data set is now split it into training and tests so now I have an official actual test set. Every time that you want to publish a paper you have some work done you have some novel model and novel data set but you could say is that you were able to model for this specific data set and you're sure that your accuracy of your model and the test data test data that was held out from the entire training process is number X and that's your contribution to the field and exactly what's happening is that if other papers or other researchers are going to do come and do exactly the same kind of research. leaf_chunk.audio: I'll do exactly the same thing. Train on the training part and then evaluate on the validation set that I have and then we are recording all of these validation errors and then cross validation error at the end is going to be the average of all of these validation errors that we had across all of those five models that we have had. So why is it helpful? Why am I doing this? It's important because now what's happening is that my data set is now split it into training and tests so now I have an official actual test set. Every time that you want to publish a paper you have some work done you have some novel model and novel data set but you could say is that you were able to model for this specific data set and you're sure that your accuracy of your model and the test data test data that was held out from the entire training process is number X and that's your contribution to the field and exactly what's happening is that if other papers or other researchers are going to do come and do exactly the same kind of research.

children.title: Cross-Validation Techniques and Project A2 Overview
children.summary: Cross-validation is a model training technique where the dataset is split into multiple rounds, with each round using a different validation fold, typically 25% of the training data. This results in different models and validation errors, with the average error reported as the cross-validation error, a crucial metric for performance. Concerns about test data bias are addressed, emphasizing the importance of separating training and test datasets to avoid variability in results. In Project A2, students will receive unlabeled training and test data to model and submit predictions, with their performance evaluated based on the test data's complexity. The significance of cross-validation for model selection is highlighted, particularly in determining optimal hyperparameters through K-fold cross-validation.

children.title: Cross-Validation in Model Training
children.summary: Cross-validation is a technique used in model training where the entire training dataset is divided into multiple rounds. In this example, four rounds are used, with each round having a validation fold that constitutes 25% of the training data. Each round produces a different model due to variations in the training data used, leading to different model parameters. The average of the validation errors from these four models is reported as the cross-validation error, which is a key metric for model performance.

In this example, what you're seeing is four, it means that my validation... Fold was 25% of my training data and then I have four full cross validation. I'll have four models every time that I want to tell you my cross validation error. That's an average of the validation errors across four different models. And notice that I have four different models. Why? Because my training data here is this set. And then my training data in the second round is going to be this other set. children.title: Cross-Validation and Test Data Bias
children.summary: The optimizer generates different parameters leading to four models and corresponding validation sets, resulting in four distinct validation errors. The cross-validation error is the average of these errors. There is a concern about potential bias in test data, which can lead to variability in results. It's noted that in practice, test data is often provided separately, avoiding fold classification between training and test datasets, as highlighted in Project A2, which follows Project A1.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So the optimizer is going to come up with different parameters. So four different models, four different validation sets. Obviously you will get four different validation errors. So cross validation error is going to be the average of all of that. There was a question somewhere here. This I resolve. Yes. A set up one more time. There was a question about, is there a possibility that our test data is going to be biased in any way? That sometimes we might get lucky, sometimes we might not get lucky. That is true. It might happen. But what we do is that we also don't do kind of fold classification between training and test. In practice a lot of time, the test data is provided to you separately. So for example, what you will see in Project A2, you're right now working on Project A1. Video: 1: Lec 16 - DS100 Fa24 - Cross-Va X

children.title: Project A2 Model Evaluation Process
children.summary: In Project A2, students will receive training and test data without labels. They are required to model the data and submit predictions, after which their model's performance will be evaluated. The test data is designed to encapsulate the complexities intended for modeling, serving as a benchmark for generalizability to unseen datasets. It is emphasized that while the test data is carefully curated, students should also practice separating training and testing data in their projects, acknowledging that this may not always be ideal.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: In Project A2, we will give you the training, the test data, but we will not give you the labels, right? And then what we'll have all of the researchers like you to do is that we'll have you to do your modeling and then submit your predictions on all of the test data points to us and then we will basically give you the performance of your model on the test data. And the person that created the test data really makes sure that the test data captures all of the complexities that we want to model for. So that's why it's like always going to be that benchmark thing that you can make sure that if you get a number and someone gets a better number, their value is going to be, or their model is going to be a better model in terms of being generalizable to all unseen data set. So it's most of the time curated, that's the summary. It's curated and carefully curated, but in kind of a practice setting that you're working on different projects, just assume that you do train tests as well as separately, and just be willing to assume that it's just going to be representative of the training day, that although it's not ideal. children.title: Cross Validation for Model Selection
children.summary: Cross validation is used for parameter selection, helping to determine optimal hyperparameters such as learning rates and model complexity. The process involves evaluating different models, such as polynomials of varying degrees, by performing K-fold cross validation to assess cross validation error and identify the best-performing model.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Very good question, thank you. Yes. Very good question. I wasn't sure which slide I should talk about that. Because that's a very kind of important point. The question is that... I have four models here, which one of those models are going to be my model at the end. So effectively what you use cross validation for is going to be for parameter selection. So for example, if you want to figure out what learning rate is going to be good, because those hyperparameters are the things that you really need to decide yourself, or if you need to decide about the model complexity. So all of those things are going to be the things that we're going to decide on based on the cross validation. So for example, I will say that... I have degree one polynomial two polynomial three and four right which one of them are going to be better. I will run. K full cross validation with degree one polynomial with degree two polynomial and so on and so forth and then I'll take a look at cross validation error. Video: 1: Lec 16 - DS100 Fa24 - Cross-Va X

children.title: Hyperparameter Tuning and Cross-Validation Techniques
children.summary: Hyperparameter tuning involves selecting the degree of polynomial with the minimum cross-validation error for the final model, which is trained on the entire dataset after validation. Hyperparameters, such as learning rates or polynomial degrees, are user-defined values that cannot be determined through traditional optimization methods. Cross-validation is a key technique for selecting these hyperparameters by testing various options and recording their cross-validation errors. For example, different learning rates like 0.1, 1, and 10 can be evaluated. The optimal hyperparameter value is chosen based on the minimum cross-validation error, and once determined, the model is trained on the full dataset before testing on the test set. The process of computing mean squared error (MSE) during cross-validation is illustrated, highlighting the number of computations required when evaluating multiple polynomial orders.

children.title: Hyperparameter Tuning and Model Training
children.summary: The degree polynomial with the minimum cross-validation error is selected for the final model, which is trained on the entire dataset after validation. Hyperparameters are defined as values that must be decided by the user, with no direct method to determine the best settings, such as learning rates or bandwidths. Cross-validation is emphasized as a technique for hyperparameter selection.

children.title: Hyperparameter Selection and Cross-Validation
children.summary: Hyperparameters are values in a model chosen before fitting to data, and they cannot be solved through calculus or optimization techniques like OLS or gradient descent. Cross-validation is utilized for selecting hyperparameters by testing various options, such as different learning rates or polynomial degrees, and recording the corresponding cross-validation errors. The model complexity or hyperparameter that results in the minimum cross-validation error is selected. For instance, if testing learning rates, candidate values like 0.1, 1, and 10 might be evaluated.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: or the kernel of what's and things like that previously and be always called it hyper parameter because we cannot directly solve it. A degree polynomial is also another one of that but what we will do is we will actually try to do cross validation on some of the options that we have for example for different learning race for different degree polynomials and then you just take a look at the cross validation error and then pick the model complexity or pick the hyper parameter with the minimum Um. with the minimum cross validation error so for example in this case what we will do is we will list out all of the possible guesses that you have for the grip polynomial or for for example learning rate and then we will run cross validation for every option we record the cross validation error and then we will pick the model or the combination that gives us the minimum cross validation error So for example, if I'm assuming that learning rate is my hyperparameter and I have some candidate options that I want to test, I would say that I'm going to test with learning rate of 0.1, 1 and 10. children.title: Cross-Validation for Hyperparameter Tuning
children.summary: To select the best hyperparameter value, perform cross-validation with various guesses for the hyperparameter, such as different learning rates. For instance, using a learning rate of 0.1 and applying three-fold cross-validation, record the cross-validation error for each guess. The hyperparameter value that results in the minimum cross-validation error is selected. After determining the optimal value, combine all training data and conduct one round of training using this value before testing on the test set.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So I will run cross validation with the learning rate of 0.1, right? Again, train validation is bullet, assume that this is three-fold cross validation, right? So I have three different faults, and I will record the cross validation error. You will do exactly the same thing with the different learning rate and with the different learning rate. And up until the point that you tested for all of the different learning rates that you have or all of the different values of the hyperparameter that you have, what you will do, I'll move this up slightly, is that you will look at those cross validation errors, and the one with the minimum value is going to be the winner. That's what we will use. What you'll do afterwards is that next time you will come, combine all of these data points together, meaning that you have one training data, now you will do one round of... training on the entire set with the learning rate of point 1 and then you will report that. and then you will test it on the test set. Test set is always waiting for you out there to just be used exactly once. Video: 1: Lec 16 - DS100 Fa24 - Cross-Va X

leaf_chunk.audio: So I will run cross validation with the learning rate of 0.1, right? Again, train validation is bullet, assume that this is three-fold cross validation, right? So I have three different faults, and I will record the cross validation error. You will do exactly the same thing with the different learning rate and with the different learning rate. And up until the point that you tested for all of the different learning rates that you have or all of the different values of the hyperparameter that you have, what you will do, I'll move this up slightly, is that you will look at those cross validation errors, and the one with the minimum value is going to be the winner. That's what we will use. What you'll do afterwards is that next time you will come, combine all of these data points together, meaning that you have one training data, now you will do one round of... training on the entire set with the learning rate of point 1 and then you will report that. and then you will test it on the test set. Test set is always waiting for you out there to just be used exactly once.

children.title: Hyperparameter Tuning and Cross-Validation
children.summary: To determine the optimal polynomial order as a hyperparameter, the segment discusses the process of computing mean squared error (MSE) during 4-fold cross-validation. With five possible polynomial orders, the total number of MSE computations required is 20. This is illustrated through an example of selecting hyperparameter values by running cross-validation for each guess and choosing the one with the lowest cross-validation error.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So, now this is the slide of question for you, the first one. It is asking you to... Think about polynomial order as your hyperparameter and it says that there are five possible options for the polynomial order and it says that if you want to run four full class validation. How many times do you need to compute mean squared error? I think that's all of you for today. We need to calculate 20 MSIs. Video: 1: Lec 16 - DS100 Fa24 - Cross-Va >

children.title: Cross-Validation, Regularization, and Model Complexity
children.summary: The segment discusses calculating Mean Squared Errors (MSEs) in cross-validation to select the best polynomial degree using 4-fold cross-validation, resulting in 20 MSE calculations. It introduces regularization for model optimization, emphasizing the control of model complexity to manage variance, with techniques such as L1 (LASSO) and L2 (Ridge) regularization. The importance of balancing linear regression to avoid overfitting and underfitting is highlighted, aiming for a 'sweet spot' that minimizes validation error. Additionally, it covers how model complexity can be restricted by imposing budget constraints on parameters, ensuring they remain below a threshold to prevent large values that negatively impact predictions. This promotes competition among features, driving some parameters to zero, and discusses the exclusion of parameter theta zero from regularization, along with navigating the loss landscape using a heat map.

children.title: Cross-Validation, Regularization, and Model Complexity
children.summary: The segment covers calculating Mean Squared Errors (MSEs) in cross-validation, specifically for selecting the best polynomial degree from five options using 4-fold cross-validation, resulting in 20 MSE calculations. It introduces regularization as a concept in model optimization, emphasizing the importance of controlling model complexity to manage variance. Techniques such as L1 (LASSO) and L2 (Ridge) regularization are discussed as methods to constrain model parameters. Additionally, it highlights the balance needed in linear regression to avoid overfitting and underfitting, aiming for a 'sweet spot' that minimizes validation error by keeping parameter sizes small.

children.title: Calculating MSIs in Cross-Validation
children.summary: To determine the best polynomial degree from five options using 4-fold cross-validation, a total of 20 Mean Squared Errors (MSEs) need to be calculated. This is because for each of the five options, four MSEs are computed during each run of cross-validation. It's important to understand whether questions are asking about the total MSEs for all runs or just for a single run of cross-validation to accurately answer them.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So 20 MSIs because you're always competing MSIs under validations. So in each one of them, when you have four full cross validation, that means that for every run of cross validation, for every option of those five possible options that you have, you need to calculate four MSIs and validations. That one, validations that two, validations that three and four. So four times per option, and then you have five options, so that is going to be 20. So these questions sometimes can be tricky. Sometimes they might ask you that per cross validation, how many MSIs you need to calculate. Or for just selecting the entire, the best possible option for, for example, learning rate or degree polynomial, how many MSIs you need to calculate. You just need to make sure that you are understanding whether it's asking about one run of clause validation or all runs of clause validation to make the final selection. children.title: Introduction to Regularization and Cross-Validation
children.summary: The discussion includes the calculation of mean squared error (MSE) during cross-validation, specifically for selecting the best polynomial degree from five options using 4-fold cross-validation, resulting in a total of 20 cases to compute. The segment also briefly touches on regularization as a model concept.

children.title: Model Complexity and Regularization Techniques
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the importance of controlling model complexity in optimization, particularly in relation to variance. As model complexity increases, variance also tends to increase, influenced by the size of the parameters. The segment emphasizes the need to constrain models to prevent them from exceeding certain boundaries, which involves implementing conditions that limit complexity. Techniques such as L1 (LASSO) and L2 (Ridge) regularization are introduced as methods to constrain model parameters and manage complexity effectively.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: It's a very important kind of topic and it also a lot of math heavy. Some of the things that we use is going to be beyond the scope for data 100 mostly. It's going to be the topic of optimization courses such as X 127, but I'm going to kind of in At the high level tell you about what's happening and how we're trying to control for the complexity of the model. Again remember that controlling the complexity means that we want to find this suite of spot. The way that we find this suite of spot is that somehow we need to constrain the model. So constrain the model means that I want to really make sure that I don't let my model cross this boundary. And what condition do I need to put in there that my model doesn't cross that boundary and doesn't get more complex? So how to make sure that the complexity is constrained? So what you're noticing here is that as the model complexity increases, also the variance is going to increase. Right? And when we are saying that the variance is increasing, one factor that contributes to increasing variance is the size of the parameters. Video: 1: Lec 16 - DS100 Fa24 - Cross

leaf_chunk.audio: It's a very important kind of topic and it also a lot of math heavy. Some of the things that we use is going to be beyond the scope for data 100 mostly. It's going to be the topic of optimization courses such as X 127, but I'm going to kind of in At the high level tell you about what's happening and how we're trying to control for the complexity of the model. Again remember that controlling the complexity means that we want to find this suite of spot. The way that we find this suite of spot is that somehow we need to constrain the model. So constrain the model means that I want to really make sure that I don't let my model cross this boundary. And what condition do I need to put in there that my model doesn't cross that boundary and doesn't get more complex? So how to make sure that the complexity is constrained? So what you're noticing here is that as the model complexity increases, also the variance is going to increase. Right? And when we are saying that the variance is increasing, one factor that contributes to increasing variance is the size of the parameters.
