children.title: Controlling Model Complexity in Linear Regression
children.summary: Model complexity must be carefully chosen to avoid overfitting, where the model memorizes training data too closely, and underfitting, where it fails to utilize available features. The goal is to find a 'sweet spot' that minimizes validation error. To control complexity, it is important to keep the size of model parameters small, as large parameters can lead to high variance due to significant changes in predictions from small changes in feature values.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: parameters because if, for example, I have a theta value that's large and it's multiplied by a feature value. By your small changes in the feature value, there is going to be a lot of changes in the impact of this term entirely, which again, if you're thinking about linear regression, having multiple different terms, it's going to substantially change the prediction. The substantially changing in prediction means that it will make the model variance high. the small changes in the feature leads to large changes in the production, leading to high variance. So one way to control model complexity or the fact that we need to do control the model complexity is just to make sure that the size of the parameters are small. So this is kind of a linear regression that we had had before. That's kind of the general gist. In order to control the model complexity, I need to make sure that the contribution of each one of the, these features that I have in this linear regression model is going to be as small as possible. children.title: Restricting Model Complexity through Regularization
children.summary: Model complexity can be controlled by imposing budget constraints on parameters to prevent them from becoming excessively large, which can adversely affect predictions. Regularization techniques limit the size of model parameters through a budget value Q, ensuring the absolute values of parameters remain below this threshold. This process promotes competition among features, driving some parameters to zero and effectively eliminating those features from consideration. The summation of absolute values of parameters is calculated to enforce this constraint, with an emphasis on not starting the index from zero to avoid biases in the dataset. Additionally, it is noted that the parameter theta zero does not influence model variance, allowing it to remain unconstrained in budget calculations.

children.title: Restricting Model Complexity with Budget Constraints
children.summary: To prevent model parameters from becoming excessively large and affecting predictions, a budget can be defined for the model. This budget ensures that the sum of all parameters does not exceed a certain limit, promoting competition among features. Consequently, some parameters may be driven to zero, effectively eliminating those features from the model, as a parameter of zero means that the corresponding feature will not contribute to predictions.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Or I just need to make sure that these parameters don't grow so large that then when I multiply it with the, with the feature with the small change in the feature, it will drastically change the prediction that we have. So one way to actually implement this idea of making sure that the parameters are small is to make sure that we define a budget and we say that the model has this total budget that it can distribute across the front features. And this will ensure that all of the features are going to compete against each other and make sure that on average or in the total kind of sense the sum of all of those parameters is going to not exceed the budget that we have. If we do that, then effectively that means that some of these theta i's are going to be as as small as possible, meaning that some of them are going to be driven into zero, which entirely is going to eliminate that feature. So if a parameter is equal to zero, that means that if you even have that feature, it's not going to, and you have it in your data frame, is going to be multiplied with the parameter 0, which means that it's going to be eliminated. children.title: Regularization and Model Complexity Control
children.summary: Regularization is a technique used to constrain model complexity by limiting the size of model parameters. This is achieved by implementing a budget, denoted as value Q, which ensures that the absolute values of the parameters remain below this threshold. By restricting how large each parameter can be, the contribution of each feature to the model is minimized, leading to a simpler model. When parameters approach zero, the complexity of the model decreases as the features have minimal impact on predictions.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: When you have that feature eliminated, that means that the number of features are smaller, so the model complexity is going to get smaller as well. That's the trick. There are multiple different things or tricks that we're thinking about in order to make sure that the model complexity stays in a constrained area, and so that it doesn't cross that boundary that I talked about. This idea is called regularization. We're putting a regulation on what's happening with the parameters and how it can actually make sure that the complexity is constrained in a certain way. The way that we're implementing this regularization or we're enforcing that regulation is that I'm going to use this concept of the budget. So I'm going to call that budget a value Q. And then I would say that I want to make sure that some of the absolute values of all of the parameters is always less than that budget Q. So this is kind of generally trying to make sure that the parameters are going to compete for this budget so they can grow independently or they can be large. children.title: Regularization and Model Complexity Constraints
children.summary: Regularization involves constraining the complexity of a model by limiting the magnitudes of its parameters. Specifically, if one parameter is large, it can force others to zero, thereby reducing the effective number of features contributing to predictions. The regulation is implemented by calculating the summation of the absolute values of all parameters, denoted as theta i, for i from 1 to p, where p is the number of features. The discussion highlights the importance of not starting the index from 0 to account for potential biases in the dataset or the need for zero-centering.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So the maximum value that each one of the is going to be q, so they can grow, so this is kind of going to constrain the complexity as well. And another hand, if one of the parameters is going to be large, it will drive the rest of them equal to zero. So again, the complexity is going to be constrained, a limited number of parameters are going to, or features are going to be contributing to the model prediction. So the implementation of this regulation is going to be actually this equation. That I will go ahead and calculate the summation of the absolute values of all of these parameters, teta i for i from 1 up to p. I have p different features. And notice here that I'm not starting from 0. I'm starting from 0. 1. And the reason that I'm not starting from 0 is that I just want to make sure that if there is some bias in my data set or if I need to 0 center my data set. Video: 1: Lec 16 - DS100 Fa24 - Cross-Va X

children.title: Restricting Model Complexity in Regularization
children.summary: In regularization, model complexity is controlled by limiting the magnitudes of the model parameters, denoted as theta i. It is emphasized that theta zero does not influence model variance, allowing it to remain unconstrained in budget calculations. The goal is to ensure that the absolute values of the parameters from 1 to P do not exceed a specified budget, Q, promoting the use of features in moderation within the model.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I don't need to penalize model for that so irrespective of whatever. Whatever the biosterm or tetasero value that we have we just believe it there we're not going to penalize model we're not going to count it towards the model complexity another reason for not including tetasero is if you take a look at my rationale in the entire equation right there was that I want to make sure that these parameters are small because they're going to be multiplied by the features. So that changes in the features are going to actually lead to large changes in the prediction. But here there is no term that's going to be multiplied with theta zero. So it's not going to influence the variance of the model. That's why we can just leave it intact. We can just not include it in the budget calculation at all. So that we're not arbitrarily constraining in something that doesn't need to be constrained. So starting from 1 up to P, absolute values, calculating absolute values of theta i's should be less than or equal to our budget. Video: 1: Lec 16 - DS100 Fa24 - Cross-Va X

children.title: Regularization and Parameter Constraints in Models
children.summary: The segment discusses the relationship between model parameters and their constraints within a budget, excluding TETA zero from regularization. It introduces a heat map of the loss landscape, demonstrating how to navigate towards optimal points by adjusting parameters TETO 1 and TETO 2. Regularization is defined as a method to limit model complexity by restricting parameter magnitudes, ensuring minimal contribution from each feature. The budget constraint in parameter optimization is visualized as a diamond shape on a graph, with parameters theta one and theta two needing to remain within this boundary. The optimal parameters must minimize loss while adhering to the budget Q, leading to potential trade-offs with training accuracy to enhance validation performance and avoid overfitting.

children.title: Regularization and Model Complexity Constraints
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the relationship between parameters in a model, specifically how they are constrained to remain within an allocated budget. TETA zero is excluded from regularization calculations. A heat map of the loss landscape is introduced, illustrating how to navigate towards an optimal point by adjusting two parameters, TETO 1 and TETO 2. Regularization is explained as a method to restrict model complexity by limiting the magnitudes of the model parameters, which ensures that each feature contributes minimally to the model.

Audio: But now what you're seeing is that what's happening is that with this kind of calculation, each one of those TETAIs are going to be constrained. They have some relationship with each other in order to make sure that they remain less than the allocated budget. And the last line here basically mentions explicitly that TETA zero is not going to be part of the regularization kind of calculation that we have. So let's see how I can enforce that regulation. So remember this lost landscape we had in this example, for simplicity, I'm assuming that I have two parameters, TETO 1 and TETO 2. And then this lost landscape is actually kind of a heat map, it's a contour map that you're seeing that as we go to the darker blue areas, the lost value is going to decrease. So meaning that if you want to run great in the sun, you will start from somewhere randomly, And then you want to make your way down to this optimum point at the center of this dark blue area. children.title: Budget Constraints in Parameter Optimization
children.summary: The discussion focuses on enforcing a budget constraint in parameter optimization, specifically stating that the absolute values of parameters theta one and theta two must be less than or equal to a budget q. This constraint is visualized as a diamond shape on a graph, where the sides represent positive and negative values of q. The size of the diamond adjusts based on the budget, determining the acceptable values for theta one and theta two that remain within this boundary. Additionally, the concept of visualizing the loss surface in gradient descent is mentioned, emphasizing the goal of finding parameter combinations that minimize loss.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So that's the tetahat kind of combination, which is the tetahat, tetahat, one hadn't tetatuhat, that is going to be optimum, which will lead to the minimum loss that you will have across the entire trainings that you have. So what's happening is that if I want to actually enforce the budget calculation that I had in mind, so this is the budget kind of constraint, I want to say that theta one, the absolute value of theta one plus absolute value of theta two, should be less than or equal to q, right? And that equation actually translates to this diamond. And kind of the four sides of this diamond, this is actually going to be q, this is going to be q, this is going to be negative q and this is also going to be negative q. Right? So you're seeing that all of the points, all of the options that I'll have for tetra1 and tetra2 are going to stay within that diamond thing. So depending on my budget, the diamond is going to grow or shrink and then I can only accept the tetra1 and tetra2 values that are within my diamond. Video: 1: Lec 16 - DS100 Fa24 - Cross-Va X

children.title: Constraining Model Parameters in Regularization
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the need to select values for parameters that minimize loss while adhering to a constraint defined by a budget Q. The optimal point of the model parameters must lie within a specific region, represented as a diamond shape on a contour map. Regularization involves constraining the sum of the absolute values of the parameters, which may lead to a trade-off with training accuracy to ensure better performance on validation data.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But at the same time, we have this other objective that we need to satisfy. We need to make sure that we picked a to one and a to two values that have a small loss or have the darkest blue color possible and this kind of contour map that we have, which essentially is going to be this exact point right on the edge or on the corner of this diamond that we're looking at. So what you're seeing is that every time that I want to regularize the model, The optimum point of the teta had to regularize with the regulation that I have the absolute value of teta i's, the sum of absolute value of teta i's, be less than. The budget Q is going to be different than the tetahedric, the tetahedric without any regularization. So this is kind of the thing that we're seeing. We are going to kind of sacrifice from the accuracy of the model on the training data. This is accuracy on the training data because we want to make sure that the model is constrained, because we want to make sure that this model is actually going to perform and the validation data as well. children.title: Constraining Model Complexity for Optimal Performance
children.summary: The discussion emphasizes the importance of constraining model complexity to avoid overfitting to training data. It highlights that while a more complex model may yield better performance on training data, it is crucial to stop training early to ensure the model remains less complex. This approach aims to improve performance on validation data, even if it results in lower training performance. The segment also touches on the concept of applying constraints to model parameters to find the closest approximation to the true minimum loss.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So this is kind of noting that if we take a look at Sorry to go back. If you take a look at this point here, you can assume that, okay, if you had the training data and you could just basically go ahead and crazily make your model complex, the optimum point of your model could be somewhere here, right? That's basically kind of the complexity that will give you your TETA hat, not regularize. Right? And what you're seeing is that that point is not going to be really an optimum point. That's the point that you are overfitted to the training data. What I need to do is I need to make sure that I stopped the training early, meaning that my model is not complex. And I stop actually at this specific point. This is the specific point that I'll do the performance on the training data is not going to be as good, because this is where we're comparing compared to this other one. But we are thinking that if it constraints the model complexity, the performance is going to be good on the validation data. Video: 1: Lec 16 - DS100 Fa24 - Cross-Va X

children.title: Model Complexity and Impact of Q on Validation
children.summary: Validation data is essential for assessing model performance on test data, where sacrificing some training performance can improve validation outcomes. A balance between underfitting and overfitting is crucial to minimize validation error. The size of Q directly affects model complexity; larger Q values lead to simpler models, while smaller Q values result in more complex models. Constraints on Q restrict permissible parameter combinations, influencing the ability to minimize loss. A very large Q can yield optimal parameters without regularization, whereas a very small Q may cause underfitting, potentially driving all parameters to zero and resulting in a constant model.

children.title: Model Complexity and Validation Data Importance
children.summary: Validation data is crucial as it indicates how well the model will perform on test data. Sacrificing some model performance on training data can lead to better validation performance. A smaller budget for model complexity can lead to underfitting, moving the optimum point further from the actual optimum. It's essential to find a balance between underfitting and overfitting, aiming for a 'sweet spot' in model complexity to minimize validation error.

children.title: Impact of Q on Model Complexity
children.summary: The segment discusses the relationship between the size of Q and model complexity. It is explained that a larger Q leads to simpler models, while a smaller Q results in more complex models. Additionally, changing the value of Q alters the region of allowed parameter combinations, affecting the model parameters that minimize loss.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I just want to make sure that it is simplified enough. The budget is small enough that is constraining something but at the same time large enough that it doesn't underfit and it exactly fits to the actual trend in the data. also take a look at the next concept. children.title: Impact of Q on Model Complexity
children.summary: The size of Q significantly influences model complexity. A small Q leads to simpler models due to increased constraints, while a large Q allows for more complex models as it provides more budget for parameter growth. The discussion emphasizes the importance of choosing an appropriate Q to effectively manage model complexity. Under the constraint of Q being greater than 10 and less than or equal to Q, only specific parameter combinations are permissible, which helps in approaching the true minimum loss.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: meaning that you're constraining it more and more so it's getting simplified and simplified and simplified up to the point that if it's too small then it's going to underfit if the q is going to be large then it can get larger larger larger and more complex because you're effectively not putting if you make q so large effectively you're not putting any constraint on the model because there's just so much budget that you can distribute to all of the parameters that they can grow large and then the model can be complex So this size of the queue is very important. We talked about the effect of using this queue or the budget in terms of making sure that we are controlling the model complexity. Again, if the queue is very large, at some point it can include the TETA hat not regularize option within itself. So there is also kind of the thing that you want to make sure that The budget queue actually is meaningful and you can use it meaningfully. So now let's take a look at this question. What happens in the extreme case if the Q is very small? Video: 1: Lec 16 - DS100 Fa24 - Cross-Va X

children.title: Impact of Q Size on Model Complexity
children.summary: When Q is very large, it can yield an optimal theta hat without regularization, while a very small Q may lead to underfitting. In cases where the model is overly constrained, all parameters may be driven to zero, resulting in a constant model or a model that always returns zero if no intercept is included. The relationship between the size of Q and model complexity indicates that small Q leads to simpler models, while large Q allows for more complex models. Additionally, applying constraints on Q restricts parameter combinations to specific ranges.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: When the Q was very large it could actually give me my optimum teta hat without regularization. If the Q is very, very small, what do you think the model will return? I'll wait until this number gets to 100 and we'll move on. Go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go, go Yay, okay. It can give me a constant model because we did not regularize on the tetasera. So if we're constraining it so much that it drives all of the parameters to be equal to zero, then I'll be left with the tetasera or the bias term. So it can be a constant model. It can also be a model that always returns zero if my model doesn't have any y-intercept or any tetasera. So again, if If there is no intercept term, you're always going to get the value of 0. If you have an intercept, you will always going to get to 0 as the output. And that's kind of the oversimplification of the model that you reduce the complexity so much that now you're literally underfitting. Video: 1: Lec 16 - DS100 Fa24 - Cross-Va X

children.title: L1 Regularization and Optimization Techniques
children.summary: The discussion covers L1 regularization as a hyperparameter in optimization to prevent underfitting and overfitting by adding a constraint to minimize mean squared error (MSE). It explains the effect of the budget parameter Q, where a small Q leads to simpler models with smaller parameter values, while a large Q allows for more complex models. The optimization process utilizes Lagrangian duality to combine mean score error and a regularization term influenced by a lambda parameter, which adjusts the importance of the budget constraint. A larger lambda favors simplicity, potentially at the cost of flexibility, while a smaller lambda enhances fit to training data. The relationship between Q and regularization is emphasized, noting that a large Q minimizes the regularization effect, resembling ordinary least squares (OLS), whereas a small Q enforces strict constraints leading to a constant model.

children.title: L1 Regularization and Budget in Optimization
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the concept of a budget as a hyperparameter in optimization to avoid underfitting and overfitting. It introduces L1 regularization, describing it as an additional constraint on the objective function, which aims to minimize mean squared error (MSE) while adhering to this new regulation. The impact of varying the budget parameter Q is explained, indicating that a small Q results in smaller parameter values, leading to a simpler model, while a large Q allows for larger parameter values, making the model more complex.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: You did not capture any pattern in your data that you wanted. So this is kind of the balance that we're trying to strike. We want to make sure that we are deciding on a budget. And now you can think about this budget as a hyperparameter that we need to set ahead of time so that we can make sure that we're not underfitting, we're not overfitting, where we can actually find the optimum point in the middle of this graph. So this brings me to the actual optimization problem. This budget function that I was writing, it was L1. L one norm of the tetavector. So effectively this is called L one regularization. What we have is that we had our own previous msc calculation for as our objective function, but this time we're saying that we want to make sure that we have low msc such that we also meet this other condition. So now we're adding this additional term or additional constraint or regulation to our objective function. Video: 1: Lec 16 - DS100 Fa24 - Cross-Va X

children.title: Optimization with Lagrangian Duality
children.summary: The objective is to achieve the least mean score error across all training data points while adhering to a budget constraint. This optimization problem can be transformed using Lagrangian duality into a combined term consisting of the mean score error and a regularization term. The regularization term is influenced by a lambda parameter, which adjusts the importance of the budget constraint in the objective function. When the budget (Q) is very large, the restriction has minimal effect, allowing for solutions similar to the ordinary least squares (OLS) approach.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: that we need to make sure that our objective right now is having least mean score error or minimum mean score error that we can have across all the training data points under the condition that we should also meet the condition of this budget constraint that we have. This is the part that is kind of beyond this scope for data 100. We can actually turn this optimization problem that has two pieces, one piece as a function and one piece as a condition, using Lagrangian duality into one specific term or one combined term. So this combined term actually has two pieces. It has one piece that relates to just the minuscord error that we had from before. And then this condition is going to be combined within this objective function using this lambda parameter. It will basically tell you that if you have this queue as your budget, I'm going to incorporate it by an importance factor here that I would say that the importance factor of the regularization term or the regularizer term or the second term is going to be that it's going to be multiplied by lambda. children.title: Objective Function and Regularization in Models
children.summary: The objective function consists of two components: one focuses on achieving a good fit for the training data, while the other aims to limit model complexity. The hyperparameter lambda controls the trade-off between these two aspects; a larger lambda indicates a preference for simplicity, potentially reducing the model's flexibility, while a smaller lambda allows for a better fit to the training data. If lambda approaches zero, the regularization effect is diminished. The discussion also highlights that with a very large value of Q, the regularization has minimal impact, allowing for solutions akin to ordinary least squares (OLS). Conversely, a very small Q leads to a constant model due to strict constraints.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And that's why the entire objective function is going to be the combination of these two pieces together. So now our objective function has two parts. One part is trying to make sure that you have a good fit on the training data. And the other part really tries to make sure that your model is not complex. And again, as you can see, this hyper parameter lambda that we have right here is going to control how much do you want to value the complexity or controlling the complexity over how much do you want to value a good production. What happens is that this lambda is actually going to be kind of one over your budget, meaning that if the queue was large, remember that when the queue was large, the diamond was growing, so this is effectively meaning that you don't want to constrain it so much, meaning that this lambda is going to be a small value. To the extent that this lambda can even be zero, just removing the impact of this regularizer term entirely. Video: 1: Lec 16 - DS100 Fa24 - Cross-Va >

children.title: L1 Regularization and Lambda Parameter Effects
children.summary: Increasing the Lambda value in L1 regularization leads to a greater penalty for larger theta values, which constrains the parameters during the optimization process. This results in a compromise between the two terms of the objective function, where a higher Lambda may negatively impact model predictions but helps optimize overall performance. L1 regularization is also known as loss of regression.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So that's why budget and this Lambda parameters are having kind of an inverse relationship. But if you increase the Lambda value, so if you want to keep adding value to this second term, making sure that your optimization process, your gradient descent is going to pay more attention and really make sure that the parameters are constrained, you want to increase the Lambda value. And then what it needs to do is this entire objective function, these two terms are going to actually play a compromise with each other. It will say that I'm going to be penalized so much if I pick a large theta value. So it will come at the cost of the model prediction, going to be not so great, but still the two together are going to be optimized overall. So that's how we're going to do this kind of calculation because the second term that we added is kind of L1 norm of of the tetah vector. This kind of regularization is called L1 regularization. And it also refers to as loss of regression or loss of regularization, which extends for... Video: 1: Lec 16 - DS100 Fa24 - Cross-Va X

children.title: Regularization Techniques in Linear Models
children.summary: The Least Absolute Sharing Kit and Selection Operator enables loss regression in linear models using the LM.lossa function, which minimizes mean squared error (MSE) and the sum of absolute parameter values. It discusses model complexity, balancing underfitting and overfitting as feature count changes, and introduces a regularization parameter alpha (lambda) set to 0.1. Shrinkage effects on horsepower-related features are explored, demonstrating how varying lambda values influence parameter selection. The segment addresses challenges in comparing feature parameters due to scale differences, emphasizing feature standardization for effective model budget allocation. L1 regularization is introduced for coefficient reduction and feature contribution optimization, while L2 regularization is discussed for controlling model complexity. The relationship between L1 and L2 regularization is noted, along with Lagrangian duality for balancing data fit and complexity in ridge regression.

children.title: Least Absolute Sharing Kit and Regularization Techniques
children.summary: The Least Absolute Sharing Kit and Selection Operator enables loss regression within the linear model framework using the LM.lossa function, which minimizes mean squared error (MSE) alongside the sum of absolute parameter values. When an intercept term is absent, parameters default to zero; if included, predictions rely solely on the intercept. The segment discusses model complexity, emphasizing the balance between underfitting and overfitting as feature count varies. The loss model, defined as Lm.dot.loss, incorporates a regularization parameter alpha (lambda), set to 0.1, for fitting the model on training data. Shrinkage in regularization is explored, particularly for horsepower-related features, aiming to limit absolute parameter values and mitigate the influence of smaller parameters. The effects of varying lambda values on shrinkage are illustrated, showing how a larger lambda (e.g., 10) reduces the parameter budget, leading to some parameters being zeroed out and thus selecting specific features.

children.title: Least Absolute Sharing Kit and Model Complexity
children.summary: The Least Absolute Sharing Kit and Selection Operator allows for the implementation of loss regression in the linear model framework, specifically using the function LM.lossa. This function minimizes mean squared error (MSE) while also minimizing the sum of the absolute values of the parameters. It is important to note that when the model does not include an intercept term, the parameters are effectively set to zero. Conversely, if an intercept term is included, the predicted value Ŷ is solely based on the intercept. The discussion also touches on model complexity, highlighting the trade-offs between underfitting and overfitting as the complexity level changes, particularly in relation to the number of features.

And LM.lossa is going to actually automatically apply loss regression, meaning that it will use the objective function that has one term minimizing msc in another term, minimizing the sum of the absolute values of the tetaparameters that you have. children.title: Shrinkage in Regularization Techniques
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the concept of shrinkage in regularization, specifically in the context of model parameters for features related to horsepower. It explains that shrinkage aims to keep the absolute values of parameters below a specified budget, which reduces the impact of features with small parameters. The segment also mentions experimenting with different values of lambda, illustrating how varying lambda affects the shrinkage process, such as comparing a larger value like 10 to a smaller one like 0.1.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Um. So take a look. I had eight different features, different powers of horsepower and you're seeing that what I have is a bunch of different parameters for tetra for each one of those features that I have and some of these parameters are pretty small. And this is kind of what we refer to as shrinkage. It will try to make sure that all of those parameters are small enough that the total when they share a budget, the total sum of those absolute values is less than the budget that we allocated to that. That's why it will try to shrink all of those parameters. When these parameters are shrinking, effectively the impact of these features are going to be eliminated, meaning that if I have a small parameter here, If it multiplies with the feature, then the feature is not going to have that much of an impact. What we'll do is we will also try different value for lambda. So here we're saying that what if lambda is the larger value like 10 as opposed to point 1. Video: 1: Lec 16 - DS100 Fa24 - Cross-Va >

leaf_chunk.audio: Um. So take a look. I had eight different features, different powers of horsepower and you're seeing that what I have is a bunch of different parameters for tetra for each one of those features that I have and some of these parameters are pretty small. And this is kind of what we refer to as shrinkage. It will try to make sure that all of those parameters are small enough that the total when they share a budget, the total sum of those absolute values is less than the budget that we allocated to that. That's why it will try to shrink all of those parameters. When these parameters are shrinking, effectively the impact of these features are going to be eliminated, meaning that if I have a small parameter here, If it multiplies with the feature, then the feature is not going to have that much of an impact. What we'll do is we will also try different value for lambda. So here we're saying that what if lambda is the larger value like 10 as opposed to point 1.

children.title: Impact of Lambda on Feature Selection
children.summary: A large lambda value in regularization penalizes the second term more, leading to a reduction in the budget for model parameters. This results in certain parameters being set to zero, effectively selecting which features to include in the model. This selection process simplifies feature selection, eliminating the need for manual parameter choice. However, a challenge arises due to the differing magnitudes of features, such as horsepower and horsepower to the power of eight, which can complicate the modeling process.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Remember that when lambda is a large value, that means that I'm going to penalize for the second term more. Meaning that your budget is now getting smaller because it had inverse relationship with the budget. So if I do exactly the same thing you're seeing that the budgets are getting smaller. So here it was, for example, it was this small value and now it's actually zero. So this is what we refer to as Selection operator because effectively when it says that one parameter is equal to zero, it's selected that that parameter should not exist or should not or if that feature should not be used in our modeling. So we'll perform feature selection for us in a sense, which is really good because we don't need to go ahead and pick and choose between different parameters or different features that we have in our modeling process. So these are the things that we had seen, but there is one issue with this kind of framework that we looked at. That in my data set, the issue was that the magnitude of the horsepower was different than the magnitude of the horsepower to the power of eight. Video: 1: Lec 16 - DS100 Fa24 - Cross-Va X

children.title: Challenges in Feature Comparison and Regularization Techniques
children.summary: The discussion covers the challenges in comparing feature parameters due to scale differences, particularly how TETA1 and TETA8 interact with varying magnitudes. It emphasizes the importance of feature standardization, which involves adjusting features to a common scale for effective budget allocation in models. L1 regularization is introduced, showing how it reduces coefficients and optimizes feature contributions. The segment also highlights feature selection's role in automating the identification of significant features and introduces L2 regularization for controlling model complexity through constraints on the L2 norm. The relationship between L1 and L2 regularization is noted, with a mention of Lagrangian duality for balancing data fit and complexity in ridge regression.

children.title: Challenges in Comparing Feature Parameters
children.summary: The discussion highlights difficulties in comparing feature parameters due to scale differences. Specifically, when substituting the TETA1 value with 0.5, it interacts with a smaller value (150), while TETA8 interacts with a much larger feature despite being a small parameter. This imbalance complicates systematic comparisons. The model aims to manage the allocated budget and balance the scale differences among features.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: You can see that there is this large value here for horsepower to the power of eight. And then these values are more reasonable when I'm going to compare the actual parameters for each one of these two features. then that comparison is not going to be meaningful. Specifically, if you take a look at how the calculation is going to look like, you're seeing that, for example, for the TETA1 value. If I replace the TETA1 value with 0.5, it is going to be multiplied with the smaller value in the scale, which is 150 compared to the case that I have this TETA8, that is this very small parameter, but then yet it's multiplied with a very large feature. So it's really kind of hard to look at those parameters or the values for those parameters and compare them systematically. Effectively, what the model is trying to do here is two things. It is trying to manage the budget that you allocated. At the same time, what it's trying to do is it tries to make sure that it balances this kind of imbalance or a scale differences that you have in the features. leaf_chunk.audio: You can see that there is this large value here for horsepower to the power of eight. And then these values are more reasonable when I'm going to compare the actual parameters for each one of these two features. then that comparison is not going to be meaningful. Specifically, if you take a look at how the calculation is going to look like, you're seeing that, for example, for the TETA1 value. If I replace the TETA1 value with 0.5, it is going to be multiplied with the smaller value in the scale, which is 150 compared to the case that I have this TETA8, that is this very small parameter, but then yet it's multiplied with a very large feature. So it's really kind of hard to look at those parameters or the values for those parameters and compare them systematically. Effectively, what the model is trying to do here is two things. It is trying to manage the budget that you allocated. At the same time, what it's trying to do is it tries to make sure that it balances this kind of imbalance or a scale differences that you have in the features.

children.title: Feature Standardization and L1 Regularization
children.summary: Standardizing features involves subtracting the mean and dividing by the standard deviation, ensuring all features are on the same scale. This allows the model to effectively allocate its budget across features. After standardization, applying L1 regularization with a parameter of 0.1 results in smaller coefficients for many parameters, indicating that the model has properly adjusted and optimized the feature contributions.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And we don't want that. Because what we can do is we can bring out of the features to the same scale so that the model actually does its own thing that it needs to do. Just distribute the budget now on the same scale features that we have. And we know we can do that with the standardization, we subtract the mean, we divide it by standard deviation, so now all of the features are going to be within the same scale. So if I go ahead and do that here, you will be able to see that, okay, I'm going to central, I'm going to standardize all of the features and now they're all going to become parable. I'm not seeing all of those really large values, but then this time if I go ahead and run lost the regression, wet the regularizer parameter 0.1, and I take a look at the coefficients, now I was able to properly do its own thing. It derived a bunch of those features, a lot of those parameters to be small. It distributed the actual budget that I gave to the model. Video: 1: Lec 16 - DS100 Fa24 - Cross-Va X

leaf_chunk.audio: And we don't want that. Because what we can do is we can bring out of the features to the same scale so that the model actually does its own thing that it needs to do. Just distribute the budget now on the same scale features that we have. And we know we can do that with the standardization, we subtract the mean, we divide it by standard deviation, so now all of the features are going to be within the same scale. So if I go ahead and do that here, you will be able to see that, okay, I'm going to central, I'm going to standardize all of the features and now they're all going to become parable. I'm not seeing all of those really large values, but then this time if I go ahead and run lost the regression, wet the regularizer parameter 0.1, and I take a look at the coefficients, now I was able to properly do its own thing. It derived a bunch of those features, a lot of those parameters to be small. It distributed the actual budget that I gave to the model.

children.title: Feature Selection and L2 Regularization Overview
children.summary: The segment discusses the importance of feature selection in model building, highlighting that it automates the process of identifying significant features without manual comparison. It then introduces L2 regularization, explaining its role in controlling model complexity by enforcing a constraint on the L2 norm of the parameter vector. The relationship between L1 and L2 regularization is noted, with a reference to how both can be framed in terms of minimizing an objective function, although the details of the Lagrangian duality are suggested for further study in a more advanced course.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And then it actually did the selection, meaning that it said that these features are not going to be really important anymore, and it will perform feature selection for us. So it's very neat what it does a lot of times we do. Lost the regression not that we're concerned with the model complexity but because we want to just do features election and we don't want to go and manually compare different kinds of. Features or come up with all combinations of features that we want to have in order to figure out which one is going to work better. So, the next thing that I want to briefly talk about is L to regularization. Every time that I talked about L1... I talked about also L2, L1 loss, L2 loss. Here we also have the same thing that I can use L2 norm of the of the tetra vector and then use the same budget but budget notion in order to control the complexity of the model. Again, the last landscape, but this time we're going to enforce the L2 to kind of equate an L2 norm of the parameter vector with the budget queue in terms of how to constrain the model. children.title: L2 and L1 Regularization in Ridge Regression
children.summary: The segment discusses the need for parameters to reside within a specific norm, particularly the L2 norm, for model regularization. It explains the use of Lagrangian duality to combine the objective function with a regularization parameter (lambda), which balances data fit and model complexity, leading to ridge regression. Additionally, it introduces L1 regularization, emphasizing the minimization of a new objective function that maintains low mean squared error (MSE) while constraining parameter sizes.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Now this time my parameters are going to need to live within the circle here, right? As opposed to the diamond thing that we have before. In fact, you can pick any kind of LP norm or any norm of the tetavectory in order to do the model to constrain the model or regularize the model. Here is L2Norm. The process is going to look like exactly the same thing that I have the MSC such that it meets the budget constrain that I have. Using Lagrangian duality again, I can combine them using this hyperparameter lambda that I have, or this lambda hyperparameter was effectively the regularizer parameter that I referred to as. And again, you can basically see that this new objective function has two terms that are going to compete with each other. How good it will fit to the data versus how model complexity is going to be constrained. And this is called ridge regression. There's actual nice implementation of Ridge regression as well. children.title: Ridge and L1 Regularization in Regression
children.summary: Ridge regression manages model complexity through a unique solution approach, ensuring a global minimum is identifiable regardless of the invertibility of X transpose times X. It employs a specific equation to achieve this. L1 regularization is introduced, focusing on minimizing a new objective function that balances low mean squared error with parameter size constraints. The regularization penalty hyperparameter, denoted as X, affects model complexity, with larger values resulting in smaller parameter estimates, and cross-validation is recommended for optimal X selection. The segment also reviews Ordinary Least Squares (OLS), emphasizing the necessity of unique solutions and regularization to prevent overfitting, while discussing training-test-validation splits and cross-validation for hyperparameter tuning. Additionally, it contrasts L1 and L2 regularization, noting that Ridge regression uses an L2 regularizer with a closed form solution, while L1 regularization does not have a closed form solution and requires a full column rank for uniqueness.

children.title: Ridge Regression and Unique Solutions
children.summary: Ridge regression controls model complexity differently than feature selection methods, ensuring a unique solution regardless of the invertibility of X transpose times X. The unique solution can be found using a specific equation, which guarantees that an optimizer can identify a global minimum. Additionally, L1 regularization is introduced, focusing on minimizing a new objective function that keeps mean squared error low while also constraining the size of parameters. The regularization penalty hyperparameter, denoted as X, influences the model's complexity, with larger values leading to smaller parameter estimates. Cross-validation is suggested as a method for selecting the optimal value of X.

Audio: So we could also go ahead and here just directly call LM.rich and calling LM.rich is going to give you all of those parameters. But you can see here is that it didn't do the feature selection that I wanted to do. That's with loss regression, with Ridge regression, it doesn't do that. It just controls the model complexity in a different way. And what you also want to know is this nice notion. Remember that I was talking about the solution to the OLS. If we said X transpose times X times X was invertible, we did have a unique solution otherwise we did not have a unique solution. With the rich regression, there is always going to be a unique solution and the solution is given by this equation. I'm not going to prove it. But the takeaway here is that If you want to have a unique solution meaning that you want to make sure that when you're running an optimizer optimizer can actually find the only single. children.title: Overview of OLS, Regularization, and Cross-Validation
children.summary: The discussion covers the importance of finding unique solutions in the Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) problem and the role of regularization in directly solving it through a regularization parameter. Key topics include an overview of OLS, training-test-validation splits, and cross-validation for hyperparameter selection. The segment emphasizes the need to constrain model complexity to avoid overfitting while ensuring reasonable performance on validation and unseen data. Additionally, L1 regularization is introduced, highlighting its objective function that minimizes mean squared error while keeping parameter sizes small, and the use of cross-validation to choose the regularization penalty hyperparameter.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So a lot of times again people are not necessarily concerned with the model complexity but they're concerned with finding a unique solution and be able to solve the OLS problem directly. In those cases, what they would do is they would actually regularize and directly solve it using this regularization parameter that is add. So this is the summary of where we are. We looked at OLS. Today we looked at training test validation splits. We talked about cross validation. We used cross validation in order to Select hyper parameters so you can actually do this in order in all of your modeling processes. And then we also complemented this all less model that we'd seen before and we saw how we can make it more constrained. Meaning that we want to make sure that the model complexity is limited so that it's not overfitting to the training data. But it has a reasonable performance on the validation data or any unseen data that we have. Video: 1: Lec 16 - DS100 Fa24 - Cross-Va X

children.title: L1 and L2 Regularization in Regression
children.summary: Rich regression utilizes an L2 regularizer and has a closed form solution, making it a popular choice. In contrast, loss regression employs an L1 regularizer and lacks a closed form solution. A unique solution for L1 regularization exists only if the matrix X is full column rank. The objective function for L1 regularization aims to minimize mean squared error while keeping the size of parameters small, with the regularization penalty hyperparameter influencing model complexity. Cross-validation is recommended for selecting the optimal value of the regularization parameter.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: In that sense, we introduced rich regression, which uses L2 regularizer. We also talked about loss regression, which uses L1 regularizer. There is no closed form solution for loss regression, but there is a closed form solution for rich regression. That's the reason a lot of times people want to use L2 or rich regression as their model. We also talked about L less previously that there is a unique solution if and only if x is full column you all for today. Good luck with the midterm tomorrow. We'll see you tomorrow.
